questions.q: In Puranic cosmology, how many yugas (world-ages) make up one complete cycle?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe four yugas: Satya, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali, which together form one great cycle.

questions.q: Which is the first and most virtuous of the four yugas in Hindu cosmology?
questions.explanation: Satya Yuga, also called Krita Yuga, is the first age, when dharma is said to stand complete on all four legs.

questions.q: Which yuga is described in the Puranas as the present, darkest age?
questions.explanation: The Puranas hold that the present age is Kali Yuga, the last and most spiritually diminished of the four ages.

questions.q: What is the Sanskrit term for the full set of four yugas taken together as one cycle?
questions.explanation: The four yugas together form a chaturyuga, also known as a mahayuga, after which the cycle renews.

questions.q: In the imagery of the yugas, dharma is often pictured as a bull standing on a number of legs. How many legs does dharma stand on during Satya Yuga?
questions.explanation: In Satya Yuga dharma is said to stand firmly on all four legs, then lose one leg in each succeeding age.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, how many legs does dharma stand upon during the present Kali Yuga?
questions.explanation: By Kali Yuga, dharma is said to stand on only one leg, reflecting the steep decline of righteousness.

questions.q: What is the correct order of the four yugas from first to last?
questions.explanation: The yugas proceed in declining order: Satya, then Treta, then Dvapara, and finally Kali.

questions.q: By what other name is the first age, Satya Yuga, commonly known?
questions.explanation: Satya Yuga is also called Krita Yuga, meaning the age that is 'accomplished' or whole in virtue.

questions.q: Across the succession of yugas, what general trend does dharma (righteousness) follow according to the Puranas?
options: It vanishes entirely in the first age
questions.explanation: The Puranas teach that dharma steadily diminishes from age to age, from four legs in Satya Yuga to one in Kali Yuga.

questions.q: After the four yugas complete their cycle, what does Puranic tradition say happens to the cosmos?
options: It is renewed and the cycle begins again
It freezes in Kali Yuga permanently
questions.explanation: After a chaturyuga, the cosmos is renewed and the cycle of ages begins anew, reflecting cyclic Hindu time.

questions.q: The Puranas state that the present Kali Yuga began at the close of which figure's earthly life?
questions.explanation: Puranic tradition holds that Kali Yuga began at the close of Krishna's earthly life, after the Mahabharata war.

questions.q: The events of the Ramayana, in which Rama appears, are traditionally placed in which yuga?
questions.explanation: Tradition places Rama and the events of the Ramayana in Treta Yuga, the second of the four ages.

questions.q: The events of the Mahabharata, in which Krishna appears, are traditionally placed in which yuga?
questions.explanation: Tradition places Krishna and the Mahabharata in Dvapara Yuga, the third of the four ages.

questions.q: Which yuga's very name is connected with the number 'four' (krita/catur), reflecting dharma standing on four legs?
questions.explanation: Satya Yuga, the age of complete dharma, is also called Krita Yuga and is associated with dharma's four legs.

questions.q: In the names Treta and Dvapara, the syllables 'tri' and 'dva' point to which idea about dharma in those ages?
options: Dharma stands on three then two legs
questions.explanation: The names reflect dharma standing on three legs in Treta Yuga and two legs in Dvapara Yuga.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, the four yugas are described as having which relative durations?
options: Each progressively longer, Kali the longest
Each progressively shorter, Satya the longest
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe each successive yuga as shorter than the last, with Satya Yuga the longest and Kali the shortest.

questions.q: In Puranic reckoning, the four yugas stand in a proportional ratio. Which ratio is traditionally given for Satya : Treta : Dvapara : Kali?
questions.explanation: The Puranas give the durations of the yugas in the ratio 4 : 3 : 2 : 1, Satya being longest and Kali shortest.

questions.q: In the Bhagavata and other Puranas, the avatara expected to appear at the end of Kali Yuga to restore dharma is named what?
questions.explanation: The Puranas name Kalki as the avatara who appears at the close of Kali Yuga to renew dharma and begin a new cycle.

questions.q: A thousand chaturyugas (cycles of four ages) are said in the Puranas to make up one of which vast cosmic period?
questions.explanation: The Puranas describe a kalpa, a day of Brahma, as comprising a thousand chaturyugas.

questions.q: Which quality is most associated with Satya Yuga in the Puranas?
questions.explanation: Satya Yuga, whose name means 'truth', is described as an age of complete virtue and truthfulness.

questions.q: In Puranic teaching, the recommended spiritual practice especially suited to the present Kali Yuga is which of the following?
options: Devotion and chanting of the divine name (nama-japa)
questions.explanation: The Puranas commend devotion and chanting of the divine name as the path best suited to Kali Yuga.

questions.q: The cyclic rise and fall of the yugas reflects which broader Hindu understanding of time?
options: Time is strictly linear with no repetition
Time moves in repeating cycles of creation and renewal
questions.explanation: The yugas express the Hindu vision of time as cyclic, with ages recurring through creation, decline, and renewal.

questions.q: In the standard Puranic figures, Kali Yuga is said to last how many years?
questions.explanation: The Puranas give Kali Yuga a span of 432,000 years, the shortest of the four ages.

questions.q: Using the traditional Puranic figures, what is the total length of one complete chaturyuga (mahayuga)?
questions.explanation: Summing the four ages (1,728,000 + 1,296,000 + 864,000 + 432,000) gives 4,320,000 years for one chaturyuga.

questions.q: In Puranic cosmology, a period ruled by one Manu, comprising many chaturyugas, is given which name?
questions.explanation: A manvantara is the reign of a single Manu, made up of many chaturyugas within a kalpa.

questions.q: The dharma-bull whose legs diminish across the yugas is, in the Bhagavata Purana, associated chiefly with which deity?
questions.explanation: The Bhagavata Purana presents the bull as Dharma personified, losing a leg in each succeeding age.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, each yuga is preceded and followed by twilight transitional periods. What are these intervals called?
questions.explanation: Each yuga has twilight intervals called sandhya (dawn) and sandhyamsha (dusk) bracketing the age proper.

questions.q: In the traditional Puranic figures, which yuga is given a duration of 1,296,000 years?
questions.explanation: Treta Yuga is given a span of 1,296,000 years, three times the length of Kali Yuga.

questions.q: In the Bhagavata Purana, which four virtues are said to be present in full during Satya Yuga and to decline thereafter?
questions.explanation: The Bhagavata Purana names austerity, purity, compassion, and truthfulness as the four legs of dharma, whole in Satya Yuga.

questions.q: In the cycle of yugas, the Kalki avatara's appearance at the end of Kali Yuga most directly leads to which outcome?
options: The permanent end of all ages
The restoration of dharma and the dawn of a new Satya Yuga
The merging of all four yugas into one
questions.explanation: The Puranas teach that Kalki restores dharma at the close of Kali Yuga, ushering in a renewed Satya Yuga and a fresh cycle.
